intro: March 2000. You're the Blockbuster CEO and you're literally undefeated — 9,000 stores, $800M a year in late fees, market cap $5B+. Then some guy named Reed pulls up asking for a meeting about his little DVD-mail side quest.

realOutcome.summary: Blockbuster filed for bankruptcy in 2010 and got fully liquidated by 2014. Every 'what actually happened' below is 100% real, no cap, in order.

decisions.scenario: Reed Hastings flies to Dallas and offers to sell you Netflix for $50M, proposing to run it as your online arm. Your execs start laughing before he finishes the sentence.

choices.text: Buy him out. $50M is lunch money, secure the bag.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Blockbuster.com launches on Netflix's own engine years early and the streaming wars never happen because there's only one king. GG.

choices.text: Laugh him out of the building, DVD-mail is a joke
choices.reveal: This is literally what happened. Antioco's team called the $50M offer a joke and sent Reed packing. Netflix is now worth roughly 400x what you refused to pay for it. Certified L.

choices.text: Counter with 'nah keep the brand, just gimme the tech'
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Netflix takes your money, keeps building the exact same company under their own name anyway. You just funded your own final boss.

decisions.scenario: Late fees are printing $800M a year, roughly a third of your entire profit. Customers despise them with their whole chest. Netflix has zero.

choices.text: Kill late fees company-wide today, no notes
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You eat the earnings hit early and rebuild the whole brand on trust, torching Netflix's best talking point years before it can hurt you.

choices.text: Keep milking it, it's a third of our bag
choices.reveal: This is exactly what happened — until 2005, when you finally caved. Five years too late, and it cost a $630M settlement over fees buried in the fine print. Rent free in the lawyers' heads.

choices.text: Rename it 'convenience fee' and hope nobody notices
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Nobody's fooled for one second. Customers clock the rebrand instantly and the backlash is worse than just admitting it was a cash grab. Ratio'd.

decisions.scenario: 2005: activist investor Carl Icahn joins the board demanding cost cuts and dividends now. Your CEO Antioco wants to keep pouring money into the online fight. Boardroom beef, live and uncut.

choices.text: Ride with Antioco, keep funding the online war
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Sustained investment means Blockbuster Online actually has the infrastructure to compete when streaming pops off, instead of getting gutted right before the fight matters.

choices.text: Side with Icahn, Antioco's out, bring in a 7-Eleven guy
choices.reveal: This is exactly what happened. Antioco resigned in 2007, replaced by Jim Keyes, who said in 2008: 'Netflix, Redbox, and Video On Demand don't scare us.' Two years later, bankruptcy. He was cooked and didn't know it.

choices.text: Split the difference, half-cut costs, half-keep the bet
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The compromise pleases literally nobody. Icahn keeps pushing, the online team gets starved anyway, and you've engineered the worst version of both strategies.

decisions.scenario: 2007: your 'Total Access' program (swap online rentals for free in-store exchanges) is actually working. You're eating Netflix's growth alive for the first time ever. Problem — it's bleeding cash and Wall Street is big mad about the margins.

choices.text: Send it. Full marketing budget into Total Access
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Reed Hastings himself later called Total Access 'the single biggest threat' Netflix ever faced. Pressing here in 2007 could've ended the whole war on the spot.

choices.text: Pull back, the shareholders want their bag now
choices.reveal: This is exactly what happened. You scaled back Total Access marketing in late 2007 to protect quarterly earnings, right as Netflix stock cratered 75%. It fully recovered within a year. You blinked at the worst possible second.

choices.text: Spin Total Access off as its own little company
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The spinoff fragments your one real advantage, splits the customer base in half, and hands Netflix a weaker, more confused version of you to beat instead.

decisions.scenario: 2010: foot traffic is in freefall, debt's due, streaming already happened without you. Dish Network is circling like it's about to happen for real.

choices.text: Full pivot to streaming before the cash hits zero
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A real streaming bet in 2010, backed by your brand and stores for a hybrid play, actually gives you a shot against a Netflix that hadn't locked the market up yet.

choices.text: File Chapter 11 and pray somebody buys the scraps
choices.reveal: This is exactly what happened. Blockbuster filed for bankruptcy in September 2010. Dish Network bought what was left in 2011 for $320M — under 1% of your peak value. Absolutely cooked.

choices.text: Sell the DVDs, go full landlord mode with the real estate
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Congrats, you're now a mid-tier commercial landlord with a shrinking lease book and no actual company. Technically alive longer. Nobody's proud of you though.

bands.line: You out-executed history itself. Villain episode: WON. No notes.
Limping but alive. Dish still buys you, just later and for more. Mid.
Faithful to history down to the letter. That's exactly the problem. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Netflix 400x'd off this exact fork. Today's agents just flagged a company sitting at a similar crossroads →
